questions.question_text: Find the volume and surface area of a cuboid 16 m long, 14 m broad and 7 m high.
questions.solution:
Volume = $(16 \times 14 \times 7) \text{ m}^3 = 1568 \text{ m}^3$.
Surface area = $[2 (16 \times 14 + 14 \times 7 + 16 \times 7)] \text{ cm}^2 = (2 \times 434) \text{ cm}^2 = 868 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: A room is 12 metres long, 9 metres broad and 8 metres high. Find the length of the longest bamboo pole that can be placed in it.
questions.solution:
Length of the longest pole = Length of the diagonal of the room
$= \sqrt{(12)^2 + 9^2 + 8^2} \text{ m} = \sqrt{289} \text{ m} = 17 \text{ m}$.

questions.question_text: The volume of a wall, 5 times as high as it is broad and 8 times as long as it is high, is 12.8 cu. metres. Find the breadth of the wall.
questions.solution:
Let the breadth of the wall be $x$ metres.
Then, Height = $5x$ metres and Length = $40x$ metres.
$\therefore x \times 5x \times 40x = 12.8 \iff x^3 = \frac{12.8}{200} = \frac{128}{2000} = \frac{64}{1000}$.
So, $x = \frac{4}{10} \text{ m} = \left(\frac{4}{10} \times 100\right) \text{ cm} = 40 \text{ cm}$.

questions.question_text: Find the number of bricks, each measuring 24 cm $\times$ 12 cm $\times$ 8 cm, required to construct a wall 24 m long, 8m high and 60 cm thick, if 10% of the wall is filled with mortar?
questions.solution:
Volume of the wall = $(2400 \times 800 \times 60) \text{ cu. cm}$.
Volume of bricks = 90% of the volume of the wall
$= \left(\frac{90}{100} \times 2400 \times 800 \times 60\right) \text{ cu. cm}$.
Volume of 1 brick = $(24 \times 12 \times 8) \text{ cu. cm}$.
$\therefore$ Number of bricks = $\left(\frac{90}{100} \times \frac{2400 \times 800 \times 60}{24 \times 12 \times 8}\right) = 45000$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular sheet of paper, 10 cm long and 8 cm wide has squares of side 2 cm cut from each of its corners. The sheet is then folded to form a tray of depth 2 cm. Find the volume of this tray.
questions.solution:
Clearly, we have:
Length of the tray = $(10 - 2 \times 2) \text{ cm} = 6 \text{ cm}$.
Breadth of the tray = $(8 - 2 \times 2) \text{ cm} = 4 \text{ cm}$.
Depth of the tray = 2 cm.
$\therefore$ Volume of the tray = $(6 \times 4 \times 2) \text{ cm}^3 = 48 \text{ cm}^3$.

questions.question_text: Water flows into a tank 200 m $\times$ 150 m through a rectangular pipe 1.5 m $\times$ 1.25 m @ 20 kmph. In what time (in minutes) will the water rise by 2 metres?
questions.solution:
Volume required in the tank = $(200 \times 150 \times 2) \text{ m}^3 = 60000 \text{ m}^3$.
Length of water column flown in 1 min. = $\left(\frac{20 \times 1000}{60}\right) \text{ m} = \frac{1000}{3} \text{ m}$.
Volume flown per minute = $\left(1.5 \times 1.25 \times \frac{1000}{3}\right) \text{ m}^3 = 625 \text{ m}^3$.
$\therefore$ Required time = $\left(\frac{60000}{625}\right) \text{ min.} = 96 \text{ min}$.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of an open box are 50 cm, 40 cm and 23 cm. Its thickness is 3 cm. If 1 cubic cm of metal used in the box weighs 0.5 gms, find the weight of the box.
questions.solution:
Volume of the metal used in the box = External volume - Internal volume
$= [(50 \times 40 \times 23) - (44 \times 34 \times 20)] \text{ cm}^3$
$= 16080 \text{ cm}^3$.
$\therefore$ Weight of the metal = $\left(\frac{16080 \times 0.5}{1000}\right) \text{ kg} = 8.04 \text{ kg}$.

questions.question_text: The diagonal of a cube is $6\sqrt{3}$ cm. Find its volume and surface area.
questions.solution:
Let the edge of the cube be $a$.
$\therefore \sqrt{3}a = 6\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow a = 6$.
So, Volume = $a^3 = (6 \times 6 \times 6) \text{ cm}^3 = 216 \text{ cm}^3$.
Surface area = $6a^2 = (6 \times 6 \times 6) \text{ cm}^2 = 216 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: The surface area of a cube is 1734 sq. cm. Find its volume.
questions.solution:
Let the edge of the cube be $a$. Then,
$6a^2 = 1734 \Rightarrow a^2 = 289 \Rightarrow a = 17 \text{ cm}$.
$\therefore$ Volume = $a^3 = (17)^3 \text{ cm}^3 = 4913 \text{ cm}^3$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular block 6 cm by 12 cm by 15 cm is cut up into an exact number of equal cubes. Find the least possible number of cubes.
questions.solution:
Volume of the block = $(6 \times 12 \times 15) \text{ cm}^3 = 1080 \text{ cm}^3$.
Side of the largest cube = H.C.F. of 6 cm, 12 cm, 15 cm = 3 cm.
Volume of this cube = $(3 \times 3 \times 3) \text{ cm}^3 = 27 \text{ cm}^3$.
Number of cubes = $\left(\frac{1080}{27}\right) = 40$.

questions.question_text: A cube of edge 15 cm is immersed completely in a rectangular vessel containing water. If the dimensions of the base of vessel are 20 cm $\times$ 15 cm, find the rise in water level.
questions.solution:
Increase in volume = Volume of the cube = $(15 \times 15 \times 15) \text{ cm}^3$.
$\therefore$ Rise in water level = $\left(\frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Area}}\right) = \left(\frac{15 \times 15 \times 15}{20 \times 15}\right) \text{ cm} = 11.25 \text{ cm}$.

questions.question_text: Three solid cubes of sides 1 cm, 6 cm and 8 cm are melted to form a new cube. Find the surface area of the cube so formed.
questions.solution:
Volume of new cube = $(1^3 + 6^3 + 8^3) \text{ cm}^3 = 729 \text{ cm}^3$.
Edge of new cube = $\sqrt[3]{729} \text{ cm} = 9 \text{ cm}$.
$\therefore$ Surface area of the new cube = $(6 \times 9 \times 9) \text{ cm}^2 = 486 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: If each edge of a cube is increased by 50%, find the percentage increase in its surface area.
questions.solution:
Let original length of each edge = $a$. Then, original surface area = $6a^2$.
New edge = $(150\% \text{ of } a) = \left(\frac{150}{100}a\right) = \frac{3a}{2}$.
New surface area = $6 \times \left(\frac{3a}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{27}{2}a^2$.
Increase percent in surface area = $\left(\frac{\frac{15}{2}a^2}{6a^2} \times 100\right)\% = 125\%$.

questions.question_text: Two cubes have their volumes in the ratio 1 : 27. Find the ratio of their surface areas.
questions.solution:
Let their edges be $a$ and $b$. Then, $\frac{a^3}{b^3} = \frac{1}{27}$ or $\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^3$ or $\frac{a}{b} = \frac{1}{3}$.
$\therefore$ Ratio of their surface areas = $\frac{6a^2}{6b^2} = \frac{a^2}{b^2} = \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9}$, i.e., 1 : 9.

questions.question_text: Find the volume, curved surface area and the total surface area of a cylinder with diameter of base 7 cm and height 40 cm.
questions.solution:
Volume = $\pi r^2 h = \left(\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \times \frac{7}{2} \times 40\right) \text{ cm}^3 = 1540 \text{ cm}^3$.
Curved surface area = $2\pi rh = \left(2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \times 40\right) \text{ cm}^2 = 880 \text{ cm}^2$.
Total surface area = $2\pi rh + 2\pi r^2 = 2\pi r (h + r)$
$= \left[2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{7}{2} \times (40 + 3.5)\right] \text{ cm}^2 = 957 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: If the capacity of a cylindrical tank is 1848 m$^3$ and the diameter of its base is 14 m, then find the depth of the tank.
questions.solution:
Let the depth of the tank be $h$ metres. Then,
$\pi \times (7)^2 \times h = 1848 \iff h = \left(1848 \times \frac{1}{22} \times \frac{7}{7} \times \frac{1}{7}\right) = 12 \text{ m}$.

questions.question_text: 2.2 cubic dm of lead is to be drawn into a cylindrical wire 0.50 cm in diameter. Find the length of the wire in metres.
questions.solution:
Let the length of the wire be $h$ metres. Then,
$\pi \times \left(\frac{0.50}{2 \times 100}\right)^2 \times h = \frac{2.2}{1000} \iff h = \left(\frac{2.2}{1000} \times \frac{100 \times 100}{0.25 \times 0.25} \times \frac{7}{22}\right) = 112 \text{ m}$.

questions.question_text: How many iron rods, each of length 7 m and diameter 2 cm can be made out of 0.88 cubic metre of iron?
questions.solution:
Volume of 1 rod = $\left(\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{1}{100} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 7\right) \text{ cu. m} = \frac{11}{5000} \text{ cu. m}$.
Volume of iron = 0.88 cu. m.
Number of rods = $\left(0.88 \times \frac{5000}{11}\right) = 400$.

questions.question_text: A well with 14 m inside diameter is dug 10 m deep. Earth taken out of it has been evenly spread all around it to a width of 21 m to form an embankment. Find the height of the embankment.
questions.solution:
Volume of earth dug out = $\left(\frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7 \times 10\right) \text{ m}^3 = 1540 \text{ m}^3$.
Area of embankment = $\frac{22}{7} \times [(28)^2 - (7)^2] = \frac{22}{7} \times 35 \times 21 \text{ m}^2 = 2310 \text{ m}^2$.
$\therefore$ Height of embankment = $\left(\frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Area}}\right) = \frac{1540}{2310} \text{ m} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ m}$.

questions.question_text: The radii of the bases of two cylinders are in the ratio 3 : 5 and their heights are in the ratio 2 : 3. Find the ratio of their curved surface areas.
questions.solution:
Let the radii of the cylinders be $3x, 5x$ and their heights be $2y, 3y$ respectively.
Then, Ratio of their curved surface areas = $\frac{2\pi \times 3x \times 2y}{2\pi \times 5x \times 3y} = \frac{2}{5} = 2 : 5$.

questions.question_text: If 1 cubic cm of cast iron weighs 21 gms, then find the weight of a cast iron pipe of length 1 metre with a bore of 3 cm and in which thickness of the metal is 1 cm.
questions.solution:
Inner radius = $\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \text{ cm} = 1.5 \text{ cm}$,
Outer radius = $(1.5 + 1) \text{ cm} = 2.5 \text{ cm}$.
$\therefore$ Volume of iron = $[\pi \times (2.5)^2 \times 100 - \pi \times (1.5)^2 \times 100] \text{ cm}^3$
$= \frac{22}{7} \times 100 \times [(2.5)^2 - (1.5)^2] \text{ cm}^3 = \frac{8800}{7} \text{ cm}^3$.
$\therefore$ Weight of the pipe = $\left(\frac{8800}{7} \times \frac{21}{1000}\right) \text{ kg} = 26.4 \text{ kg}$.

questions.question_text: Find the slant height, volume, curved surface area and the whole surface area of a cone of radius 21 cm and height 28 cm.
questions.solution:
Here, $r = 21 \text{ cm}$ and $h = 28 \text{ cm}$.
$\therefore$ Slant height, $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{(21)^2 + (28)^2} = \sqrt{1225} = 35 \text{ cm}$.
Volume = $\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \left(\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 21 \times 21 \times 28\right) \text{ cm}^3 = 12936 \text{ cm}^3$.
Curved surface area = $\pi rl = \left(\frac{22}{7} \times 21 \times 35\right) \text{ cm}^2 = 2310 \text{ cm}^2$.
Total surface area = $(\pi rl + \pi r^2) = \left(2310 + \frac{22}{7} \times 21 \times 21\right) \text{ cm}^2 = 3696 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: A conical tent is required to accommodate 5 persons and each person needs 16 cm$^2$ of space on the ground and 100 cubic metres of air to breathe. Find the vertical height of the tent.
questions.solution:
Let the radius of the base of the tent be $r$ and the vertical height be $h$.
Then, area of the base = $\pi r^2$.
$\therefore \pi r^2 = 16 \times 5 = 80 \Rightarrow r^2 = \frac{80}{\pi}$.
Volume of the tent = $\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times \frac{80}{\pi} \times h = \frac{80h}{3}$.
$\therefore \frac{80h}{3} = 100 \times 5 \Rightarrow 80h = 1500 \Rightarrow h = \frac{75}{4} = 18\frac{3}{4}$.
Hence, height of the tent = $18\frac{3}{4} \text{ m}$.

questions.question_text: How many metres of cloth 5 m wide will be required to make a conical tent, the radius of whose base is 7m and height is 24 m?
questions.solution:
Here, $r = 7 \text{ m}$ and $h = 24 \text{ m}$.
So, $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{7^2 + (24)^2} = \sqrt{625} = 25 \text{ m}$.
Area of canvas = $\pi rl = \left(\frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 25\right) \text{ m}^2 = 550 \text{ m}^2$.
$\therefore$ Length of canvas = $\left(\frac{\text{Area}}{\text{Width}}\right) = \left(\frac{550}{5}\right) \text{ m} = 110 \text{ m}$.

questions.question_text: The heights of two right circular cones are in the ratio 1 : 2 and the perimeters of their bases are in the ratio 3 : 4. Find the ratio of their volumes.
questions.solution:
Let the radii of their bases be $r$ and $R$ and their heights be $h$ and $2h$ respectively.
Then, $\frac{2\pi r}{2\pi R} = \frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{r}{R} = \frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow R = \frac{4}{3}r$.
$\therefore$ Ratio of volumes = $\frac{\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h}{\frac{1}{3}\pi R^2 (2h)} = \frac{r^2}{2R^2} = \frac{r^2}{2 \times (\frac{4}{3}r)^2} = \frac{9}{32} = 9 : 32$.

questions.question_text: A copper sphere of diameter 18 cm is drawn into a wire of diameter 4 mm. Find the length of the wire.
questions.solution:
Volume of sphere = $\left(\frac{4}{3}\times\pi\times9\times9\times9\right)$ cm$^3$ = $972\pi$ cm$^3$.
Volume of wire = $(\pi\times0.2\times0.2\times h)$ cm$^3$.
$\therefore 972\pi = \pi\times\frac{2}{10}\times\frac{2}{10}\times h \Rightarrow h = \left(\frac{972\times5\times5}{1}\right)$ cm = $243$ m.

questions.question_text: Two metallic right circular cones having their heights 4.1 cm and 4.3 cm and the radii of their bases 2.1 cm each, have been melted together and recast into a sphere. Find the diameter of the sphere.
questions.solution:
Volume of sphere = Volume of 2 cones = $\left[\frac{1}{3}\pi\times(2.1)^2\times4.1 + \frac{1}{3}\pi\times(2.1)^2\times4.3\right]$ cm$^3$ = $\frac{1}{3}\pi\times(2.1)^2\times(8.4)$ cm$^3$.
Let the radius of the sphere be R.
$\therefore \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{1}{3}\pi(2.1)^2\times4 \Rightarrow R = 2.1$ cm.
Hence, diameter of the sphere = 4.2 cm.

questions.question_text: A cone and a sphere have equal radii and equal volumes. Find the ratio of the diameter of the sphere to the height of the cone.
questions.solution:
Let the radius of each be R and height of the cone be H.
Then, $\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{1}{3}\pi R^2 H$ or $\frac{R}{H} = \frac{1}{4}$ or $\frac{2R}{H} = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$.
$\therefore$ Required ratio = 1 : 2.

questions.question_text: Find the volume, curved surface area and the total surface area of a hemisphere of radius 10.5 cm.
questions.solution:
Volume = $\frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{21}{2}\times\frac{21}{2}\times\frac{21}{2}\right)$ cm$^3$ = 2425.5 cm$^3$.
Curved surface area = $2\pi r^2 = \left(2\times\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{21}{2}\times\frac{21}{2}\right)$ cm$^2$ = 693 cm$^2$.
Total surface area = $3\pi r^2 = \left(3\times\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{21}{2}\times\frac{21}{2}\right)$ cm$^2$ = 1039.5 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: A hemispherical bowl of internal radius 9 cm contains a liquid. This liquid is to be filled into cylindrical shaped small bottles of diameter 3 cm and height 4 cm. How many bottles will be needed to empty the bowl?
questions.solution:
Volume of bowl = $\left(\frac{2}{3}\pi\times9\times9\times9\right)$ cm$^3$ = $486\pi$ cm$^3$.
Volume of 1 bottle = $\left(\pi\times\frac{3}{2}\times\frac{3}{2}\times4\right)$ cm$^3$ = $9\pi$ cm$^3$.
Number of bottles = $\frac{486\pi}{9\pi} = 54$.

questions.question_text: A cone, a hemisphere and a cylinder stand on equal bases and have the same height. Find the ratio of their volumes.
questions.solution:
Let R be the radius of each.
Height of hemisphere = Its radius = R.
$\therefore$ Height of each = R.
Ratio of volumes = $\frac{1}{3}\pi R^2\times R : \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3 : \pi R^2\times R = 1 : 2 : 3$.

questions.question_text: A cylindrical container of radius 6 cm and height 15 cm is filled with ice-cream. The whole ice-cream has to be distributed to 10 children in equal cones with hemispherical tops. If the height of the conical portion is four times the radius of its base, then find the radius of the ice-cream cone.
questions.solution:
Volume of ice-cream in cylindrical container = $(\pi\times6^2\times15)$ cm$^3$ = $(540\pi)$ cm$^3$.
Let the radius of the base of the cone be r cm.
Then, height of the cone = $(4r)$ cm.
Volume of ice-cream in 10 cones with hemispherical tops = $10\left[\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2\times4r + \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3\right]$ cm$^3$ = $(20\pi r^3)$ cm$^3$.
$\therefore 20\pi r^3 = 540\pi \Rightarrow r^3 = \frac{540}{20} = 27 \Rightarrow r = 3$.
Hence, radius of ice-cream cone = 3 cm.

questions.question_text: A cuboid has .......... edges.
questions.solution: The question is not provided in the text.

questions.question_text: 1 litre is equal to
questions.solution: The question is not provided in the text.

questions.question_text: A rectangular water tank is 8 m high, 6 m long and 2.5 m wide. How many litres of water can it hold?
questions.solution:
Volume of the tank = $(8\times100\times6\times100\times2.5\times100)$ $\text{cm}^3$
$=120000000$ $\text{cm}^3$
$=\left(\frac{120000000}{1000}\right)$ litres $=120000$ litres.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of a cuboid are 7cm, 11 cm and 13 cm. The total surface area is
questions.solution:
Surface area = $[2(7\times11+11\times13+7\times13)]$ $\text{cm}^2$
$=(2\times311)$ $\text{cm}^2=622$ $\text{cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: A closed aquarium of dimensions 30 cm $\times$ 25 cm $\times$ 20 cm is made up entirely of glass plates held together with tapes. The total length of tape required to hold the plates together (ignore the overlapping tapes) is
questions.solution:
Total length of tape required = Sum of lengths of edges
$=(30\times4+25\times4+20\times3)$ cm $=300$ cm.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of a room are 15 m, 10 m and 8 m. The volume of a bag is 2.25 m$^3$. The maximum number of bags that can be accommodated in the room is
questions.solution:
Required number of bags = $\frac{\text{Volume of the room}}{\text{Volume of each bag}}$
$=\frac{15\times10\times8}{2.25}\approx533.333=533$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular water reservoir contains 42000 litres of water. If the length of reservoir is 6 m and breadth of the reservoir is 3.5 m, then the depth of the reservoir will be
questions.solution:
Volume of the reservoir $=42000$ litres $=42$ $\text{m}^3$.
Let the depth of the reservoir be $h$ metres.
Then, $6\times3.5\times h=42$ or $h=\frac{42}{6\times3.5}=2$ m.

questions.question_text: A cistern 6 m long and 4 m wide contains water up to a depth of 1 m 25 cm. The total area of the wet surface is
questions.solution:
Area of the wet surface = $[2(lb+bh+lh)-lb]$
$=2(bh+lh)+lb$
$=[2(4\times1.25+6\times1.25)+6\times4]$ $\text{m}^2=49$ $\text{m}^2$.

questions.question_text: A boat having a length 3 m and breadth 2 m is floating on a lake. The boat sinks by 1 cm when a man gets on it. The mass of man is
questions.solution:
Volume of water displaced $=(3\times2\times0.01)$ $\text{m}^3=0.06$ $\text{m}^3$.
$\therefore$ Mass of man = Volume of water displaced $\times$ Density of water
$=(0.06\times1000)$ kg $=60$ kg.

questions.question_text: A water tank is 30 m long, 20 m wide and 12 m deep. It is made of iron sheet which is 3 m wide. The tank is open at the top. If the cost of the iron sheet is ₹ 10 per metre, then the total cost of the iron sheet required to build the tank is
questions.solution:
Since the tank is open at the top, we have:
Area of sheet required = Surface area of the tank
$=lb+2(bh+lh)$
$=[30\times20+2(20\times12+30\times12)]$ $\text{m}^2=(600+1200)$ $\text{m}^2=1800$ $\text{m}^2$.
Length of sheet required = $\frac{\text{Area}}{\text{Width}}=\left(\frac{1800}{3}\right)$ m $=600$ m.
$\therefore$ Cost of the sheet = ₹ $(600\times10)=$ ₹ $6000$.

questions.question_text: Given that 1 cu. cm of marble weighs 2.5 gms, the weight of a marble block 28 cm in width and 5 cm thick is 112 kg. The length of the block is
questions.solution:
Let length $=x$ cm. Then,
$x\times28\times\frac{5}{1000}=112\Rightarrow x=\left(112\times\frac{1000}{25\times28\times5}\right)$ cm $=32$ cm.

questions.question_text: Half cubic metre of gold sheet is extended by hammering so as to cover an area of 1 hectare. The thickness of the sheet is
questions.solution:
Volume of gold = $\left(\frac{1}{2}\times100\times100\times100\right)$ $\text{cm}^3$.
Area of sheet $=10000$ $\text{m}^2=(10000\times100\times100)$ $\text{cm}^2$.
$\therefore$ Thickness of the sheet = $\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}\times100\times100\times100}{10000\times100\times100}\right)$ cm $=0.005$ cm.

questions.question_text: In a shower, 5 cm of rain falls. The volume of water that falls on 1.5 hectares of ground is :
questions.solution:
Area $=(1.5\times10000)$ $\text{m}^2=15000$ $\text{m}^2$. Depth $=\frac{5}{100}$ m $=\frac{1}{20}$ m.
$\therefore$ Volume $=(\text{Area}\times\text{Depth})=\left(15000\times\frac{1}{20}\right)$ $\text{m}^3=750$ $\text{m}^3$.

questions.question_text: The breadth of a room is twice its height and half its length. The volume of the room is 512 cu. m. The length of the room is
questions.solution:
Let the height of the room be $x$ metres.
Then, breadth $=2x$ metres and length $=4x$ metres.
$\therefore$ Volume of the room $=(4x\times2x\times x)$ $\text{m}^3=(8x^3)$ $\text{m}^3$.
$8x^3=512\Rightarrow x^3=64\Rightarrow x=4$.
Length of the room is 16 m.

questions.question_text: The length of a cold storage is double its breadth. Its height is 3 metres. The area of its four walls (including the doors) is 108 m$^2$. Find its volume.
questions.solution:
Let the breadth be $x$ metres. Then, length $=2x$ metres.
Area of 4 walls $=[2(2x+x)\times3]$ $\text{m}^2=(18x)$ $\text{m}^2$.
$\therefore 18x=108\Rightarrow x=\frac{108}{18}=6$.
So, length $=12$ m, breadth $=6$ m.
Volume $=(12\times6\times3)$ $\text{m}^3=216$ $\text{m}^3$.

questions.question_text: The length of a hall is 20 metres and the width is 16 metres. The sum of the areas of the floor and roof is equal to the sum of the areas of the four walls. Find the volume of the hall.
questions.solution:
Let the height of the hall be $h$ metres.
Then, $2\times20\times16=2(20+16)\times h\Rightarrow 72h=640$
$\Rightarrow h=\frac{640}{72}=\frac{80}{9}$.
$\therefore$ Volume of the hall
$=\left(20\times16\times\frac{80}{9}\right)$ $\text{m}^3=\left(\frac{25600}{9}\right)$ $\text{m}^3=2844.4$ $\text{m}^3$.

questions.question_text: If V be the volume and S be the surface area of a cuboid of dimensions a, b, c, then $\frac{1}{V}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$V=abc$.
$S=2(ab+bc+ca)=2abc\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\right)$.

questions.question_text: The volume of a rectangular block of stone is 10368 dm$^3$. Its dimensions are in the ratio of 3 : 2 : 1. If its entire surface is polished at 2 paise per dm$^2$, then the total cost will be
questions.solution:
Let the dimensions be $3x$, $2x$ and $x$ respectively. Then,
$3x\times2x\times x=10368\Leftrightarrow x^3=\frac{10368}{6}=1728\Leftrightarrow x=12$.
So, the dimensions of the block are 36 dm, 24 dm and 12 dm.
Surface area $=[2(36\times24+24\times12+36\times12)]$ $\text{dm}^2$
$=[2\times144(6+2+3)]$ $\text{dm}^2=3168$ $\text{dm}^2$.
$\therefore$ Cost of polishing $=₹\left(\frac{3168\times2}{100}\right)=₹63.36$.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of a rectangular box are in the ratio 2 : 3 : 4 and the difference between the cost of covering it with sheet of paper at the rate of ₹ 8 and ₹ 9.50 per square metre is ₹ 1248. Find the dimensions of the box in meters.
questions.solution:
Let the length, breadth and height of the box be $2x$, $3x$ and $4x$ respectively.
Then, surface area of the box $=2[2x.3x+3x.4x+2x.4x]$
$=[2(6x^2+12x^2+8x^2)]=52x^2$.
$\therefore 52x^2=\frac{1248}{1.50}=832\Rightarrow x^2=\frac{832}{52}=16\Rightarrow x=4$.
Hence, the dimensions of the box are 8 m, 12 m and 16 m.

questions.question_text: It is required to construct a big rectangular hall to accommodate 500 persons, allowing 22.5 m$^3$ space per person. The height of the hall is to be kept at 7.5 m, while the total inner surface area of the walls must be 1200 sq. m. Then the length and breadth of the hall respectively are
questions.solution:
Volume of the hall $=(500\times22.5)$ $\text{m}^3=11250$ $\text{m}^3$.
Let the length and breadth of the hall be $l$ and $b$ metres respectively.
Then, $l\times b\times h=11250\Rightarrow lb=\frac{11250}{7.5}=1500$ ...(i)
And, $2(l+b)\times h=1200\Rightarrow 2(l+b)=\frac{1200}{7.5}=160$
$\Rightarrow l+b=80$ ...(ii)
Putting $b=(80-l)$ in (i), we get:
$l(80-l)=1500\Rightarrow l^2-80l+1500=0\Rightarrow (l-30)(l-50)=0\Rightarrow l=50$.
Hence, length $=50$ m, breadth $=30$ m.

questions.question_text: A cuboidal water tank contains 216 litres of water. Its depth is $\frac{1}{3}$ of its length and breadth is $\frac{1}{2}$ of the difference between length and depth. The length of the tank is
questions.solution:
Let the length of the tank be $x$ dm. Then, depth of the tank = $ rac{x}{3}$ dm.
Breadth of the tank
$=  rac{1}{2}$ of $ rac{1}{3}$ of $ rac{x}{3}$ dm = $ rac{x}{18}$ dm.
$\therefore x \times \frac{x}{3} \times \frac{x}{18} = 216 \Rightarrow x^3 = 216 \times 54 \Rightarrow x = 6 \times 3 = 18$.

questions.question_text: The length of the longest rod that can be placed in a room of dimensions 10 m $\times$ 10 m $\times$ 5 m is
Find the length of the longest rod that can be placed in a room 16 m long, 12 m broad and $10\frac{2}{3}$ m high.
The volume of a rectangular solid is 210 cm$^3$ and the surface area is 214 cm$^2$. If the area of the base is 42 cm$^2$, then the edges of the rectangular solid are
questions.solution:
Let $l, b$ and $h$ represent the lengths of the edges of the solid. Then, $l \times b = 42$ ...(i)
$lbh = 210 \Rightarrow h = \frac{210}{lb} = \frac{210}{42} \Rightarrow h = 5$.
Surface area = $2(lb + bh + lh) = 2(42 + 5b + 5l) = 84 + 10(l + b)$.
$\therefore 84 + 10(l + b) = 214 \Rightarrow l + b = 13$. ...(ii)
Putting $b = (13 - l)$ in (i), we get: $l(13 - l) = 42 \Rightarrow l^2 - 13l + 42 = 0 \Rightarrow (l - 6)(l - 7) = 0 \Rightarrow l = 7$.
Hence, length = $7$ cm, breadth = $6$ cm, height = $5$ cm.

questions.question_text: How many bricks, each measuring 25 cm $\times$ 11.25 cm $\times$ 6 cm, will be needed to build a wall 8 m $\times$ 6 m $\times$ 22.5 cm?
questions.solution: Number of bricks = $\frac{\text{Volume of the wall}}{\text{Volume of 1 brick}} = \left(\frac{800 \times 600 \times 22.5}{25 \times 11.25 \times 6}\right) = 6400$.

questions.question_text: The number of bricks, each measuring 25 cm $\times$ 12.5 cm $\times$ 7.5 cm, required to construct a wall 6 m long, 5 m high and 0.5 m thick, while the mortar occupies 5% of the volume of the wall, is
questions.solution:
Volume of the bricks = $95\%$ of volume of wall
$= \left(\frac{95}{100} \times 600 \times 500 \times 50\right)$ cm$^3$.
Volume of 1 brick = $(25 \times 12.5 \times 7.5)$ cm$^3$.
$\therefore$ Number of bricks = $\left(\frac{95}{100} \times \frac{600 \times 500 \times 50}{25 \times 12.5 \times 7.5}\right) = 6080$.

questions.question_text: 50 men took a dip in a water tank 40 m long and 20 m broad on a religious day. If the average displacement of water by a man is 4 m$^3$, then the rise in the water level in the tank will be :
questions.solution:
Total volume of water displaced = $(4 \times 50)$ m$^3$ = $200$ m$^3$.
$\therefore$ Rise in water level = $\left(\frac{200}{40 \times 20}\right)$ m = $0.25$ m = $25$ cm.

questions.question_text: A swimming bath is 24 m long and 15 m broad. When a number of men dive into the bath, the height of the water rises by 1 cm. If the average amount of water displaced by one of the men be 0.1 cu. m, how many men are there in the bath?
questions.solution:
Volume of water displaced = $\left(\frac{1}{100} \times 24 \times 15\right)$ m$^3$ = $\frac{18}{5}$ m$^3$.
Volume of water displaced by 1 man = $0.1$ m$^3$.
$\therefore$ Number of men = $\left(\frac{18/5}{0.1}\right) = \left(\frac{18}{5} \times 10\right) = 36$.

questions.question_text: A school room is to be built to accommodate 70 children so as to allow 2.2 m$^2$ of floor and 11 m$^3$ of space for each child. If the room be 14 metres long, what must be its breadth and height?
questions.solution:
Let the breadth and height of the room be $b$ and $h$ metres respectively.
Then, area of the floor = $(14b)$ m$^2$.
$\therefore 14b = 2.2 \times 70 \Rightarrow b = \frac{2.2 \times 70}{14} = 11$.
Volume of the room = $(14 \times 11 \times h)$ m$^3$ = $(154h)$ m$^3$.
$\therefore 154h = 11 \times 70 \Rightarrow h = \frac{11 \times 70}{154} = 5$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular tank measuring 5 m $\times$ 4.5 m $\times$ 2.1 m is dug in the centre of the field measuring 13.5 m by 2.5 m. The earth dug out is evenly spread over the remaining portion of the field. How much is the level of the field raised?
questions.solution:
Volume of earth dug out = $(5 \times 4.5 \times 2.1)$ m$^3$ = $47.25$ m$^3$.
Area over which earth is spread = $(13.5 \times 2.5 - 5 \times 4.5)$ m$^2$ = $(33.75 - 22.5)$ m$^2$ = $11.25$ m$^2$.
$\therefore$ Rise in level = $\frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Area}} = \left(\frac{47.25}{11.25}\right)$ m = $4.2$ m.

questions.question_text: A plot of land in the form of a rectangle has dimensions 240 m $\times$ 180 m. A drainlet 10 m wide is dug all around it (outside) and the earth dug out is evenly spread over the plot, increasing its surface level by 25 cm. The depth of the drainlet is
questions.solution:
Volume of earth dug out = $(240 \times 180 \times 0.25)$ m$^3$ = $10800$ m$^3$.
Let the depth of the drainlet be $h$ metres.
Then, volume of earth dug out = $[\{(260 \times 200) - (240 \times 180)\}h]$ m$^3$ = $(8800h)$ m$^3$.
$\therefore 8800h = 10800 \Rightarrow h = \frac{10800}{8800} = \frac{27}{22} = 1.227$ m.

questions.question_text: A cistern, open at the top, is to be lined with sheet of lead which weights 27 kg/m$^2$. The cistern is 4.5 m long and 3 m wide and holds 50 m$^3$. The weight of lead required is
questions.solution:
Let the depth of the cistern be $h$ metres.
Then, $4.5 \times 3 \times h = 50 \Rightarrow h = \frac{50}{13.5} = \frac{100}{27}$.
Area of sheet required = $lb + 2(bh + lh) = lb + 2h(l + b)$
$= \left[4.5 \times 3 + 2 \times \frac{100}{27} \times (4.5 + 3)\right]$ m$^2$
$= \left[13.5 + 2 \times \frac{100}{27} \times 7.5\right]$ m$^2 = \left[13.5 + \frac{500}{9}\right]$ m$^2 = \frac{1243}{18}$ m$^2$.
$\therefore$ Weight of lead = $\left(\frac{1243}{18} \times 27\right)$ kg = $\frac{3729}{2}$ kg = $1864.5$ kg.

questions.question_text: If a river 2.5 m deep and 45 m wide is flowing at the rate of 3.6 km per hour, then the amount of water that runs into the sea per minute is
questions.solution:
Length of water column flown in 1 min = $\left(\frac{3.6 \times 1000}{60}\right)$ m = $60$ m.
$\therefore$ Volume flown per minute = $(60 \times 45 \times 2.5)$ m$^3$ = $6750$ m$^3$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular water tank is 80 m $\times$ 40 m. Water flows into it through a pipe 40 sq. cm at the opening at a speed of 10 km/hr. By how much, the water level will rise in the tank in half an hour?
questions.solution:
Length of water column flown in 1 min = $\left(\frac{10 \times 1000}{60}\right)$ m = $\frac{500}{3}$ m.
Volume flown per minute = $\left(\frac{500}{3} \times \frac{40}{100} \times \frac{100}{100}\right)$ m$^3$ = $\frac{2}{3}$ m$^3$.
Volume flown in half an hour = $\left(\frac{2}{3} \times 30\right)$ m$^3$ = $20$ m$^3$.
$\therefore$ Rise in water level = $\left(\frac{20}{40 \times 80}\right)$ m = $\frac{1}{160} \times 100$ cm = $\frac{5}{8}$ cm.

questions.question_text: A rectangular tank is 225 m by 162 m at the base. With what speed must water flow into it through an aperture 60 cm by 45 cm so that the level may be raised 20 cm in 5 hours?
questions.solution:
Volume flown in 5 hours = $\left(225 \times 162 \times \frac{20}{100}\right)$ m$^3$ = $7290$ m$^3$.
Volume flown in 1 hour = $\frac{7290}{5}$ m$^3$ = $1458$ m$^3$.
$\therefore$ Required speed = $\left(\frac{1458}{0.60 \times 0.45}\right)$ m/hr = $5400$ m/hr.

questions.question_text: The water in a rectangular reservoir having a base 80 m by 60 m is 6.5 m deep. In what time can the water be emptied by a pipe of which the cross-section is a square of side 20 cm, if the water runs through the pipe at the rate of 15 km per hour?
questions.solution:
Volume of water in the reservoir = $(80 \times 60 \times 6.5)$ m$^3$ = $31200$ m$^3$.
Volume of water flowing out per hour = $\left(15000 \times \frac{20}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}\right)$ m$^3$ = $600$ m$^3$.
$\therefore$ Total time taken to empty the tank = $\frac{31200}{600}$ hrs = $52$ hrs.

questions.question_text: Rita and Meeta both are having lunch boxes of a cuboidal shape. Length and breadth of Rita’s lunch box are 10% more than that of Meeta’s lunch box, but the depth of Rita’s lunch box is 20% less than that of Meeta’s lunch box. The ratio of the capacity of Rita’s lunch box to that of Meeta’s lunch box is
questions.solution:
Let $l, b$ and $h$ denote the length, breadth and depth of Meeta's lunch box.
Then, length of Rita's lunch box = $110\%$ of $l = \frac{11}{10}l$.
Breadth of Rita's lunch box = $110\%$ of $b = \frac{11}{10}b$.
Depth of Rita's lunch box = $80\%$ of $h = \frac{4}{5}h$.
$\therefore$ Ratio of the capacities of Rita's and Meeta's lunch boxes = $\left(\frac{11}{10} \times \frac{11}{10} \times \frac{4}{5}\right) lbh : lbh = 121 : 125$.

questions.question_text: The sum of the length, breadth and depth of a cuboid is 19 cm and its diagonal is $5\sqrt{5}$ cm. It surface area is
questions.solution:
$(l + b + h) = 19$ and $(l^2 + b^2 + h^2) = 125$.
Now, $(l + b + h)^2 = 19^2 \Rightarrow (l^2 + b^2 + h^2) + 2(lb + bh + lh) = 361$
$\Rightarrow 2(lb + bh + lh) = (361 - 125) = 236$.
$\therefore$ Surface area = $236$ cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: The sum of perimeters of the six faces of a cuboid is 72 cm and the total surface area of the cuboid is 16 cm$^2$. Find the longest possible length that can be kept inside the cuboid
questions.solution:
Sum of perimeters of the six faces = $2[2(l + b) + 2(b + h) + 2(l + h)] = 8(l + b + h)$.
Total surface area = $2(lb + bh + lh)$.
$\therefore 8(l + b + h) = 72$ and $2(lb + bh + lh) = 16 \Rightarrow l + b + h = 9$ and $lb + bh + lh = 8$.
Now, $(l + b + h)^2 = l^2 + b^2 + h^2 + 2(lb + bh + lh)$
$\Rightarrow 9^2 = l^2 + b^2 + h^2 + 16 \Rightarrow l^2 + b^2 + h^2 = 81 - 16 = 65$.
Required length = $\sqrt{l^2 + b^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{65} = 8.05$ cm.

questions.question_text: A swimming pool 9 m wide and 12 m long is 1 m deep on the shallow side and 4 m deep on the deeper side. Its volume is :
questions.solution: Volume = $\left[12 \times 9 \times \left(\frac{1+4}{2}\right)\right]$ m$^3$ = $(12 \times 9 \times 2.5)$ m$^3$ = $270$ m$^3$.

questions.question_text: Length of a rectangular solid is increased by 10% and breadth is decreased by 10%. Then the volume of the solid
questions.solution:
Let the original length, breadth and height of the solid be $l$, $b$ and $h$ respectively.
Original volume = $(lbh)$ cu. units.
New length = $110\%$ of $l = \frac{11}{10}l$
New breadth = $90\%$ of $b = \frac{9}{10}b$
New volume = $\left(\frac{11}{10} \times \frac{9}{10}\right) lbh = \frac{99}{100} lbh$ cu. units.
Decrease = $\left(lbh - \frac{99}{100} lbh\right) = \frac{1}{100} lbh$
Decrease% = $\left(\frac{\frac{1}{100} lbh}{lbh} \times 100\right) \% = 1\%$.

questions.question_text: The length, breadth and height of a cuboid are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 3. The length, breadth and height of the cuboid are increased by 100%, 200% and 200% respectively. Then the increase in the volume of the cuboid is
questions.solution:
Let the original length, breadth and height of the cuboid be $x$, $2x$ and $3x$ units respectively.
Then, original volume = $(x \times 2x \times 3x)$ cu. units = $6x^3$ cu. units.
New length = $200\%$ of $x = 2x$,
New breadth = $300\%$ of $2x = 6x$,
New height = $300\%$ of $3x = 9x$.
New volume = $(2x \times 6x \times 9x)$ cu. units = $108x^3$ cu. units.
Increase in volume = $(108x^3 - 6x^3)$ cu. units = $102x^3$ cu. units.
Required ratio = $\frac{102x^3}{6x^3} = 17$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular piece of cardboard 18 cm $\times$ 24 cm is made into an open box by cutting a square of 5 cm side from each corner and building up the side. Find the volume of the box in cu. cm.
questions.solution:
Clearly, $l = (18 - 10)$ cm = $8$ cm, $b = (24 - 10)$ cm = $14$ cm, $h = 5$ cm.
Volume of the box = $(8 \times 14 \times 5)$ cm$^3$ = $560$ cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: An open box is made by cutting the congruent squares from the corners of a rectangular sheet of cardboard of dimensions 20 cm $\times$ 15 cm. If the side of each square is 2 cm, the total outer surface area of the box is
questions.solution:
Clearly, $l = (20 - 4)$ cm = $16$ cm, $b = (15 - 4)$ cm = $11$ cm, $h = 2$ cm.
Outer surface area of the box = $[2(l + b) \times h] + lb$
= $[\{2(16 + 11) \times 2\} + 16 \times 11]$ cm$^2$
= $(108 + 176)$ cm$^2$ = $284$ cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: A closed box made of wood of uniform thickness has length, breadth and height 12 cm, 10 cm and 8 cm respectively. If the thickness of the wood is 1 cm, the inner surface area is
questions.solution:
Internal length = $(12 - 2)$ cm = $10$ cm,
Internal breadth = $(10 - 2)$ cm = $8$ cm,
Internal height = $(8 - 2)$ cm = $6$ cm.
Inner surface area = $2[10 \times 8 + 8 \times 6 + 10 \times 6]$ cm$^2$
= $(2 \times 188)$ cm$^2$ = $376$ cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: A covered wooden box has the inner measures as 115 cm, 75 cm and 35 cm and the thickness of wood is 2.5 cm. Find the volume of the wood.
questions.solution:
The external measures of the box are $(115 + 5)$ cm, $(75 + 5)$ cm and $(35 + 5)$ cm i.e., $120$ cm, $80$ cm and $40$ cm.
Volume of the wood = External volume - Internal volume
= $[(120 \times 80 \times 40) - (115 \times 75 \times 35)]$ cm$^3$
= $(384000 - 301875)$ cm$^3$ = $82125$ cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of an open box are 52 cm $\times$ 40 cm $\times$ 29 cm. Its thickness is 2 cm. If 1 cu. cm of metal used in the box weighs 0.5 gm, then the weight of the box is
questions.solution:
Since the box is an open one, we have:
Internal length = $(52 - 4)$ cm = $48$ cm;
Internal breadth = $(40 - 4)$ cm = $36$ cm;
Internal depth = $(29 - 2)$ cm = $27$ cm.
Volume of the metal used in the box = External volume - Internal volume
= $[(52 \times 40 \times 29) - (48 \times 36 \times 27)]$ cm$^3$
= $(60320 - 46656)$ cm$^3$ = $13664$ cm$^3$.
Weight of the box = $\left(\frac{13664 \times 0.5}{1000}\right)$ kg = $6.832$ kg.

questions.question_text: An open box is made of wood 3 cm thick. Its external dimensions are 1.46 m, 1.16 m and 8.3 dm. The cost of painting the inner surface of the box at 50 paise per 100 sq. cm is
questions.solution:
Internal length = $(146 - 6)$ cm = $140$ cm.
Internal breadth = $(116 - 6)$ cm = $110$ cm.
Internal depth = $(83 - 3)$ cm = $80$ cm.
Area of inner surface = $[2(l + b) \times h] + lb$
= $[2(140 + 110) \times 80 + 140 \times 110]$ cm$^2$ = $55400$ cm$^2$.
Cost of painting = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 55400\right)$ = ₹ $277$.

questions.question_text: A cistern of capacity 8000 litres measures externally 3.3 m by 2.6 m by 1.1 m and its walls are 5 cm thick. The thickness of the bottom is
questions.solution:
Let the thickness of the bottom be $x$ cm.
Then, $[(330 - 10) \times (260 - 10) \times (110 - x)] = 8000 \times 1000$
$320 \times 250 \times (110 - x) = 8000 \times 1000$
$(110 - x) = \frac{8000 \times 1000}{320 \times 250} = 100$
$x = 10$ cm = $1$ dm.

questions.question_text: If a metallic cuboid weighs 16 kg, how much would a miniature cuboid of metal weigh, if all dimensions are reduced to one-fourth of the original?
questions.solution:
Let the dimensions of the bigger cuboid be $x$, $y$ and $z$.
Then, Volume of the bigger cuboid = $xyz$.
Volume of the miniature cuboid
= $\left(\frac{1}{4}x \times \frac{1}{4}y \times \frac{1}{4}z\right) = \frac{1}{64}xyz$.
Weight of the miniature cuboid = $\left(\frac{1}{64} \times 16\right)$ kg = $0.25$ kg.

questions.question_text: A rectangular water tank is open at the top. Its capacity is 24 m$^3$. Its length and breadth are 4 m and 3 m respectively. Ignoring the thickness of the material used for building the tank, the total cost of painting the inner and outer surfaces of the tank at the rate of ₹ 10 per m$^2$ is
questions.solution:
Depth of the tank = $\left(\frac{24}{4 \times 3}\right)$ m = $2$ m.
Since the tank is open and thickness of material is to be ignored, we have
Sum of inner and outer surfaces = $2[\{2(l + b) \times h\} + lb]$
= $2[\{2(4 + 3) \times 2\} + 4 \times 3]$ m$^2$ = $80$ m$^2$.
Cost of painting = ₹ $(80 \times 10)$ = ₹ $800$.

questions.question_text: If the areas of three adjacent faces of a cuboid are x, y, z respectively, then the volume of the cuboid is
questions.solution:
Let length = $l$, breadth = $b$, height = $h$. Then, $x = lb$, $y = bh$, $z = lh$.
Let $V$ be the volume of the cuboid. Then, $V = lbh$.
$xyz = lb \times bh \times lh = (lbh)^2 = V^2$ or $V = \sqrt{xyz}$.

questions.question_text: If the areas of the three adjacent faces of a cuboidal box are 120 cm$^2$, 72 cm$^2$ and 60 cm$^2$ respectively, then find the volume of the box.
questions.solution:
Let the length, breadth and height of the box be $l$, $b$ and $h$ respectively. Then,
Volume = $lbh = \sqrt{(lbh)^2} = \sqrt{lb \times bh \times lh} = \sqrt{120 \times 72 \times 60} = 720$ cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: If the areas of three adjacent faces of a rectangular block are in the ratio of 2 : 3 : 4 and its volume is 9000 cu. cm; then the length of the shortest side is
questions.solution:
Let $lb = 2x$, $bh = 3x$ and $lh = 4x$.
Then, $24x^3 = (lbh)^2 = 9000 \times 9000 \Rightarrow x^3 = 375 \times 9000 \Rightarrow x = 150$.
So, $lb = 300$, $bh = 450$, $lh = 600$ and $lbh = 9000$.
$h = \frac{9000}{300} = 30$, $l = \frac{9000}{450} = 20$ and $b = \frac{9000}{600} = 15$.
Hence, shortest side = $15$ cm.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of a certain machine are 48″ $\times$ 30″ $\times$ 52″. If the size of the machine is increased proportionately until the sum of its dimensions equals 156″, what will be the increase in the shortest side?
questions.solution:
Sum of original dimensions = $48 + 30 + 52 = 130$.
Increase in sum = $156 - 130 = 26$.
Since the dimensions have been increased proportionately,
so increase in shortest side = $\left(\frac{30}{130} \times 26\right) = 6$.

questions.question_text: If a metal slab of size 1 m $\times$ 20 cm $\times$ 1 cm is melted to another slab of 1 mm thickness and 1 m width, then the length of the new slab thus formed will be
questions.solution:
Let the length of the new slab be $x$ metres.
Then, $1 \times 0.20 \times 0.01 = x \times 0.001 \times 1 \Rightarrow x = \frac{0.002}{0.001} = 2$.
Required length = $2$ m = $200$ cm.

questions.question_text: Rahul hired a contractor to dig a well of 10 metres length, 10 metres breadth and 10 metres depth for ₹ 40000. However, when the contractor was about to start the work, he changed his mind and asked him to get two wells dug, each with a length of 5 metres, breadth of 5 metres and depth of 5 metres. How much should Rahul pay to the contractor?
questions.solution:
Clearly, payment shall be made in proportion to the volume of earth dug.
Volume actually dug = $(2 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5) = 250$.
Volume to be dug as settled = $(10 \times 10 \times 10) = 1000$.
Ratio = $\frac{250}{1000} = \frac{1}{4}$.
Payment to be made = $\left(\frac{1}{4} \times 40000\right) = 10000$.

questions.question_text: Each side of a cube measures 8 metres. What is the volume of the cube?
questions.solution: Volume of the cube = $8^3$ cu. m = $512$ cu. m.

questions.question_text: The perimeter of one face of a cube is 20 cm. Its volume must be
questions.solution:
Edge of the cube = $\left(\frac{20}{4}\right)$ cm = $5$ cm.
Volume = $(5 \times 5 \times 5)$ cm$^3$ = $125$ cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: Total surface area of a cube whose side is 0.5 cm is
questions.solution: Surface area = $6 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2$ cm$^2 = \frac{6}{4}$ cm$^2 = 1.5$ cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: The cost of the paint is ₹ 36.50 per kg. If 1 kg of paint covers 16 square feet, how much will it cost to paint outside of a cube having 8 feet each side?
questions.solution:
Surface area of the cube = $(6 	imes 8^2)$ sq. ft. = 384 sq. ft.
Quantity of paint required = $ rac{384}{16}$ kg = 24 kg.
$\therefore$ Cost of painting = ₹ $(36.50 	imes 24)$ = ₹ 876.

questions.question_text: If the volume of a cube is 729 cm$^3$, then the surface area of the cube will be
questions.solution:
$a^3 = 729 \Rightarrow a = \sqrt[3]{729} = 9$.
$\therefore$ Surface area = $6a^2 = (6 	imes 9 	imes 9)$ cm$^2$ = 486 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: The surface area of a cube is 150 cm$^2$. Its volume is
questions.solution:
$6a^2 = 150 \Rightarrow a^2 = 25 \Rightarrow a = 5$.
$\therefore$ Volume = $a^3 = 5^3$ cm$^3$ = 125 cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of a piece of iron in the shape of a cuboid are 270 cm $\times$ 100 cm $\times$ 64 cm. If it is melted and recast into a cube, then the surface area of the cube will be
questions.solution:
Volume of the cube = $(270 	imes 100 	imes 64)$ cm$^3$.
Edge of the cube =
$\sqrt[3]{270 \times 100 \times 64}$ cm = $(3 \times 10 \times 4)$ cm = 120 cm.
$\therefore$ Surface area = $(6 	imes 120 	imes 120)$ cm$^2$ = 86400 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: The cost of painting the whole surface area of a cube at the rate of 13 paise per sq. cm is ₹ 343.98. Then the volume of the cube is :
questions.solution:
Surface area = $\frac{34398}{13} = 2646$ cm$^2$.
$\therefore 6a^2 = 2646 \Rightarrow a^2 = 441 \Rightarrow a = 21$.
So, Volume = $(21 \times 21 \times 21)$ cm$^3$ = 9261 cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: An aluminium sheet 27 cm long, 8 cm broad and 1 cm thick is melted into a cube. The difference in the surface areas of the two solids would be
questions.solution:
Volume of cube = Volume of sheet = $(27 \times 8 \times 1)$ cm$^3$ = 216 cm$^3$.
Edge of cube = $\sqrt[3]{216}$ cm = 6 cm.
Surface area of sheet = $2(lb + bh + lh) = 2(27 \times 8 + 8 \times 1 + 27 \times 1)$ cm$^2$.
= $(216 + 8 + 27)$ cm$^2$ = 502 cm$^2$.
Surface area of cube = $6a^2 = (6 \times 6^2)$ cm$^2$ = 216 cm$^2$.
$\therefore$ Required difference = $(502 - 216)$ cm$^2$ = 286 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: The length of an edge of a hollow cube open at one face is 3 metres. What is the length of the largest pole that it can accommodate?
questions.solution: Required length = Diagonal = $\sqrt{3}a = (\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{3})$ m = 3 m.

questions.question_text: What is the volume of a cube (in cubic cm) whose diagonal measures $4\sqrt{3}$ cm?
questions.solution:
$\sqrt{3}a = 4\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow a = 4$.
$\therefore$ Volume = $(4 \times 4 \times 4)$ cm$^3$ = 64 cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: If the total length of diagonals of a cube is 12 cm, then what is the total length of the edges of the cube?
questions.solution:
Since a cube has 4 diagonals, we have: Length of a diagonal = $\frac{12}{4}$ cm = 3 cm.
Let the length of each edge of the cube be $a$ cm.
Then, $\sqrt{3}a = 3$ or $a = \sqrt{3}$.
$\therefore$ Total length of the edges of the cube = $12\sqrt{3}$ cm.

questions.question_text: If the surface area of a cube is 13254 cm$^2$, then the length of its diagonal is
questions.solution:
$6a^2 = 13254 \Rightarrow a^2 = 2209 \Rightarrow a = \sqrt{2209} = 47$.
$\therefore$ Length of diagonal = $\sqrt{3}a = 47\sqrt{3}$ cm.

questions.passage_text:
$V_1, V_2, V_3$ and $V_4$ are the volumes of four cubes of side lengths x cm, 2x cm, 3x cm and 4x cm respectively. Some statements regarding these volumes are given below
(1) $V_1 + V_2 + 2V_3 < V_4$
(2) $V_1 + 4V_2 + V_3 < V_4$
(3) $2(V_1 + V_3) + V_2 = V_4$
Which of these statements are correct?
questions.question_text: Which of these statements are correct?
From a cube of side 8m, a square hole of 3m side is hollowed from end to end. What is the volume of the remaining solid?
questions.solution:
Volume of the remaining solid = Volume of the cube -
Volume of the cuboid cut out from it
= $[(8 \times 8 \times 8) - (3 \times 3 \times 8)]$ m$^3$ = $(512 - 72)$ m$^3$ = 440 m$^3$.

questions.question_text: If the numbers representing volume and surface area of a cube are equal, then the length of the edge of the cube in terms of the unit of measurement will be
questions.solution: $a^3 = 6a^2 \Rightarrow a = 6$.

questions.question_text: The volume of a cube is numerically equal to the sum of its edges. What is its total surface area in square units?
questions.solution:
$a^3 = 12a \Rightarrow a^2 = 12 \Rightarrow 6a^2 = (6 \times 12)$ sq. units
= 72 sq. units.

questions.question_text: Except for one face of a given cube, identical cubes are glued through their faces to all the other faces of the given cube. If each side of the given cube measures 3 cm, then what is the total surface area of the solid body thus formed?
questions.solution:
Clearly, each of the 5 faces of the given cube are glued
to a face of another cube.
$\therefore$ Total surface area of the solid $5 \times 5a^2 + a^2 = 26a^2$
= $(26 \times 3^2)$ cm$^2$ = 234 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: A solid cube just gets completely immersed in water when a 0.2 kg mass is placed on it. If the mass is removed, the cube is 2 cm above the water level. What is the length of each side of the cube?
questions.solution:
Let the length of each side of the cube be $a$ cm.
Then, volume of the part of cube outside water = volume
of the mass placed on it
$\Rightarrow 2a^2 = 0.2 \times 1000 = 200 \Rightarrow a^2 = 100 \Rightarrow a = 10$.

questions.question_text: A cube of length 1 cm is taken out from a cube of length 8 cm. What is the weight of the remaining portion?
questions.option_a: $\frac{7}{8}$ of the weight of the original cube
questions.option_b: $\frac{8}{9}$ of the weight of the original cube
questions.option_c: $\frac{63}{64}$ of the weight of the original cube
questions.option_d: $\frac{511}{512}$ of the weight of the original cube
questions.solution:
Volume of the bigger cube = $(8^3)$ cm$^3$ = 512 cm$^3$.
Volume of the cut-out cube = $(1^3)$ cm$^3$ = 1 cm$^3$.
Volume of the remaining portion = $(512 - 1)$ cm$^3$
= 511 cm$^3$.
Weight of the remaining portion
Weight of the original cube = $\frac{511}{512}$.

questions.question_text: How many cubes of 10 cm edge can be put in a cubical box of 1 m edge?
questions.solution: Number of cubes = $\frac{100 \times 100 \times 100}{10 \times 10 \times 10} = 1000$.

questions.question_text: A 4 cm cube is cut into 1 cm cubes. The total surface area of all the small cubes is
questions.solution:
Number of small cubes formed = $\frac{4 \times 4 \times 4}{1 \times 1 \times 1} = 64$.
Total surface area of the small cubes = $[64 \times (6 \times 1^2)]$ cm$^2$
= 384 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular block with a volume of 250 cm$^3$ was sliced into two cubes of equal volume. How much greater (in sq. cm) is the combined surface area of the two cubes then the original surface area of the rectangular block?
questions.solution:
Clearly, when the rectangular block was cut into 2 identical cubes, two new faces were formed - one on each cube
along the line of the cut. So, the difference in surface areas
is equal to the surface area of the newly formed faces.
Volume of each cube = $\frac{250}{2}$ cm$^3$ = 125 cm$^3$.
Edge of each cube = $\sqrt[3]{125}$ cm = 5 cm.
Hence, difference in surface areas = $(2 \times 5^2)$ cm$^2$ = 50 cm$^2$.

questions.question_text: A rectangular box measures internally 1.6 m long, 1 m broad and 60 cm deep. The number of cubical blocks each of edge 20 cm that can be packed inside the box is
questions.solution: Number of blocks = $\left(\frac{160\times100\times60}{20\times20\times20}\right) = 120$.

questions.question_text: How many cubes of 3 cm edge can be cut out of a cube of 18 cm edge?
questions.solution: Number of cubes = $\left(\frac{18\times18\times18}{3\times3\times3}\right) = 216$.

questions.question_text: Shobhraj takes a cube of 1 m edge-length and meticulously cuts smaller cubes, each of edge-length 1 mm from the parent cube. He joins these small cubes end-to-end. Thus, the total length of this ‘cube-robe’ will be
questions.solution:
Number of cubes formed = $\frac{3\times3\times3}{1\times1\times1} = 10\times10\times10 = 9\times10^9$.
\ Total length of cube-robe = $(1\times10^9)$ mm = $10^9$ mm
= $\left(\frac{10^9}{10^6}\right)$ km = $10^3$ km = 1000 km.

questions.question_text: How many small cubes, each of 96 cm surface area, can be formed from the material obtained by melting a larger cube of 384 cm surface area?
questions.solution:
Let the length of each edge of small cube be $a_1$ and that of large cube be $a_2$.
Then, $6a_1^2 = 96$ and $6a_2^2 = 384 \Rightarrow a_1^2 = 16$ and $a_2^2 = 64 \Rightarrow a_1 = 4$ and $a_2 = 8$.
\ Number of cubes formed = $\frac{\text{Volume of larger cube}}{\text{Volume of smaller cube}}$
= $\left(\frac{8\times8\times8}{4\times4\times4}\right) = 8$.

questions.question_text: The volume of a cuboid is twice that of a cube. If the dimensions of the cuboid are 9 cm, 8 cm and 6 cm, the total surface area of the cube is
questions.solution:
Volume of the cuboid = $(9\times8\times6)$ cm$^3$ = 432 cm$^3$.
Volume of the cube = $\left(\frac{1}{2}\times432\right)$ cm$^3$ = 216 cm$^3$.
$a^3 = 216 \Rightarrow a = \sqrt[3]{216} = 6 \Rightarrow 6a^2 = (6\times6^2) = 216$.

questions.question_text: A cuboidal block of 6 cm $\times$ 9 cm $\times$ 12 cm is cut up into an exact number of equal cubes. The least possible number of cubes will be
questions.solution:
Volume of block = $(6\times9\times12)$ cm$^3$ = 648 cm$^3$.
Side of largest cube = H.C.F. of 6 cm, 9 cm, 12 cm = 3 cm.
Volume of this cube = $(3\times3\times3)$ cm = 27 cm$^3$.
\ Number of cubes = $\left(\frac{648}{27}\right) = 24$.

questions.question_text: The size of a wooden block is 5 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 20 cm. How many such blocks will be required to construct a solid wooden cube of minimum size?
questions.solution:
Side of smallest cube = L.C.M. of 5 cm, 10 cm, 20 cm = 20 cm.
Volume of the cube = $(20\times20\times20)$ cm$^3$ = 8000 cm$^3$.
Volume of the block = $(5\times10\times20)$ cm$^3$ = 1000 cm$^3$.
\ Number of blocks = $\left(\frac{8000}{1000}\right) = 8$.

questions.question_text: An iron cube of side 10 cm is hammered into a rectangular sheet of thickness 0.5 cm. If the sides of the sheet are in the ratio 1 : 5, the sides are
questions.solution:
Let the sides of the sheet be $x$ and $5x$. Then,
Volume of the sheet = Volume of the cube
$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{1}{5}\times x\times5x\right) = 10\times10\times10 \Rightarrow 5x^2 = 2000 \Rightarrow x^2 = 400 \Rightarrow x = 20$.
\ The sides are 20 cm and 100 cm.

questions.question_text: A cube of white chalk is painted red, and then cut parallel to the sides to form two rectangular solids of equal volumes. What percent of the surface area of each of the new solids is not painted red?
questions.solution:
Let the length of each edge of the cube be $a$.
Then, the dimensions of each of the two rectangular solids are $a, a$ and $\frac{a}{2}$.
Surface area of each rectangular solid = $2\left[a\times a + a\times\frac{a}{2} + a\times\frac{a}{2}\right] = 4a^2$.
Surface area of unpainted face of each solid = $(a\times a) = a^2$.
\ Required percentage = $\left(\frac{a^2}{4a^2}\times100\right)\% = 25\%$.

questions.question_text: There is a cube of volume 216 cm$^3$. It is to be moulded into a cuboid having one edge equal to 6 cm. The number of ways that it can be done so that the edges have different integral values is
questions.solution:
Let the other two dimensions of the cuboid be $a$ and $b$ cm respectively.
Then, $6ab = 216$ or $ab = 36$.
The possible values of $(a, b)$ are (1, 36), (2, 18), (3, 12) and (4, 9).

questions.question_text: If three cubes of copper, each with an edge of 6 cm 8 cm and 10 cm respectively are melted to form a single cube, then the diagonal of the new cube will be
questions.solution:
Volume of the new cube = $(6^3 + 8^3 + 10^3)$ cm$^3$ = 1728 cm$^3$.
Let the edge of the new cube be $a$ cm.
\ $a^3 = 1728 \Rightarrow a = 12$.
Hence, length of diagonal = $\sqrt{3}a = 12\sqrt{3}$ cm = $(12\times1.732)$ cm = 20.784 cm $\approx$ 20.8 cm.

questions.question_text: A larger cube is formed from the material obtained by melting three smaller cubes of 3, 4 and 5 cm side. The ratio of the total surface areas of the smaller cubes and the larger cube is
questions.solution:
Volume of the larger cube = $(3^3 + 4^3 + 5^3)$ cm$^3$ = 216 cm$^3$.
Let the edge of the larger cube be $a$ cm.
\ $a^3 = 216 \Rightarrow a = 6$.
Required ratio = $\frac{6^2}{(3^2 + 4^2 + 5^2)} = \frac{6\times50}{6\times36} = \frac{25}{18}$.

questions.question_text: Five equal cubes, each of side 5 cm, are placed adjacent to each other. The volume of the new solid formed will be
questions.solution:
The new solid formed is a cuboid of length 25 cm, breadth 5 cm and height 5 cm.
\ Volume = $(25\times5\times5)$ cm$^3$ = 625 cm$^3$.

questions.question_text: If three equal cubes are placed adjacently in a row, then the ratio of the total surface area of the new cuboid to the sum of the surface areas of the three cubes will be
questions.solution:
Let the length of each edge of each cube be $a$.
Then, the cuboid formed by placing 3 cubes adjacently has the dimensions $3a, a$ and $a$.
Surface area of the cuboid = $2[3a\times a + a\times a + 3a\times a] = 2(3a^2 + a^2 + 3a^2) = 14a^2$.
Sum of surface areas of 3 cubes = $(3\times6a^2) = 18a^2$.
\ Required ratio = $14a^2 : 18a^2 = 7 : 9$.

questions.question_text: Three cubes with sides in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5 are melted to form a single cube whose diagonal is $12\sqrt{3}$ cm. The sides of the cubes are
questions.solution:
Let the sides of the three cubes be $3x, 4x$ and $5x$.
Then, Volume of the new cube = $[(3x)^3 + (4x)^3 + (5x)^3] = 216x^3$.
Edge of the new cube = $(216x^3)^{1/3} = 6x$.
Diagonal of the new cube = $6\sqrt{3}x$.
\ $6\sqrt{3}x = 12\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x = 2$.
So, the sides of the cubes are 6 cm, 8 cm and 10 cm.

questions.question_text: If the volumes of two cubes are in the ratio 27 : 1, the ratio of their edges is
questions.solution:
Let their edges be $a$ and $b$.
Then, $\frac{a^3}{b^3} = \frac{27}{1} \Leftrightarrow \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{3}{1}\right)^3 \Leftrightarrow \frac{a}{b} = \frac{3}{1} \Leftrightarrow a : b = 3 : 1$.

questions.question_text: The volumes of two cubes are in the ratio 8 : 27. The ratio of their surface areas is
questions.solution:
Let their edges be $a$ and $b$. Then,
$\frac{a^3}{b^3} = \frac{8}{27} \Leftrightarrow \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^3 \Leftrightarrow \frac{a}{b} = \frac{2}{3} \Leftrightarrow \frac{a^2}{b^2} = \frac{4}{9} \Leftrightarrow \frac{6a^2}{6b^2} = \frac{4}{9}$.

questions.question_text: Two cubes have volumes in the ratio 1 : 27. Then the ratio of the area of the face of one of the cubes to that of the other is
questions.solution:
Let their edges be $a$ and $b$. Then,
$\frac{a^3}{b^3} = \frac{1}{27} \Leftrightarrow \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^3 \Leftrightarrow \frac{a}{b} = \frac{1}{3} \Leftrightarrow \frac{a^2}{b^2} = \frac{1}{9}$.

questions.question_text: If each edge of a cube is doubled, then its volume:
questions.solution:
Let original edge = $a$. Then, volume = $a^3$.
New edge = $2a$. So, new volume = $(2a)^3 = 8a^3$.
\ Volume becomes 8 times.

questions.question_text: By what percent the volume of a cube increases if the length of each edge was increased by 50%?
questions.solution:
Let original edge = $a$. Then, original volume = $a^3$.
New edge = $\frac{150}{100}a = \frac{3a}{2}$. New volume = $\left(\frac{3a}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{27a^3}{8}$.
Increase in volume = $\left(\frac{27a^3}{8} - a^3\right) = \frac{19a^3}{8}$.
\ Increase% = $\left(\frac{19a^3}{8} \times \frac{1}{a^3} \times 100\right)\% = 237.5\%$.

questions.question_text: If each edge of a cube is increased by 25%, then the percentage increase in its surface area is :
questions.solution:
Let original edge = $a$. The, surface area = $6a^2$.
New edge = $ rac{125}{100}a =  rac{5}{4}a$.
New surface area = $6 	imes  rac{75}{8}a^2 =  rac{75a^2}{8}$.
Increase in surface area = $ rac{75a^2}{8} - 6a^2 =  rac{27a^2}{8}$.
Increase % = $ rac{27a^2}{8} 	imes  rac{1}{6a^2} 	imes 100 \% = 56.25\%$.

questions.question_text: A cube of edge 20 cm is completely immersed in a rectangular vessel containing water. If the dimensions of the base of the vessel are 20 cm by 40 cm, the rise in water level will be
questions.solution:
Volume increased = $(20)^3 \text{ cm}^3 = 8000 \text{ cm}^3$.
Rise in water level = $ rac{8000}{20 	imes 40} \text{ cm} = 10 \text{ cm}$.

questions.question_text: A circular well with a diameter of 2 metres, is dug to a depth of 14 metres. What is the volume of the earth dug out?
questions.solution: Volume = $\pi r^2 h =  rac{22}{7} 	imes 1 	imes 1 	imes 14 \text{ m}^3 = 44 \text{ m}^3$.

questions.question_text: Find the cost of a cylinder of radius 14 m and height 3.5 m when the cost of its metal is ₹ 50 per cubic metre.
questions.solution:
Volume = $\pi r^2 h =  rac{22}{7} 	imes 14 	imes 14 	imes 3.5 \text{ m}^3 = 2156 \text{ m}^3$.
Cost of the cylinder = ₹ $(2156 	imes 50) = ₹ 107800$.

questions.question_text: If the radius and height of a right circular cylinder are 21 cm and 35 cm respectively, then the total surface area of the cylinder is
questions.solution: Total surface area = $2\pi r(h+r) = 2 	imes  rac{22}{7} 	imes 21 	imes (35 + 21) \text{ cm}^2 = 7392 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: The capacity of a cylindrical tank is 246.4 litres. If the height is 4 metres, what is the diameter of the base?
questions.solution:
Volume of the tank = $246.4 \text{ litres} = 246400 \text{ cm}^3$.
Let the radius of the base be $r \text{ cm}$. Then,
$ rac{22}{7} 	imes r^2 	imes 400 = 246400 \Rightarrow r^2 =  rac{246400 	imes 7}{22 	imes 400} = 196 \Rightarrow r = 14$.
Diameter of the base = $2r = 28 \text{ cm}$.

questions.question_text: The volume of a right circular cylinder, 14 cm in height is equal to that of a cube whose edge is 11
questions.solution:
Volume of the cylinder = Volume of the cube = $(11)^3 \text{ cm}^3 = 1331 \text{ cm}^3$.
Let the radius of the base be $r \text{ cm}$. Then,
$ rac{22}{7} 	imes r^2 	imes 14 = 1331 \Rightarrow 44r^2 = 1331 \Rightarrow r^2 =  rac{1331}{44} =  rac{121}{4} \Rightarrow r =  rac{11}{2} = 5.5$.

questions.question_text: Capacity of a cylindrical vessel is 25.872 litres. If the height of the cylinder is three times the radius of its base, what is the area of the base?
questions.solution:
Volume of cylinder = $25.872 \text{ litres} = 25872 \text{ cm}^3$.
Let the radius of the base of the cylinder be $r \text{ cm}$. Then, height = $(3r) \text{ cm}$.
$ rac{22}{7} 	imes r^2 	imes (3r) = 25872 \Rightarrow r^3 =  rac{25872 	imes 7}{66} = 2744 \Rightarrow r = \sqrt[3]{2744} = 14$.
Hence, area of the base = $\pi r^2 =  rac{22}{7} 	imes 14 	imes 14 \text{ cm}^2 = 616 \text{ cm}^2$.

questions.question_text: Two rectangular sheets of paper, each 30 cm $\times$ 18 cm are made into two right circular cylinders, one by rolling the paper along its length and the other along the breadth. The ratio of the volumes of the two cylinders, thus formed, is
questions.solution:
Clearly, the cylinder formed by rolling the paper along its length has height $18 \text{ cm}$ and circumference of base $30 \text{ cm}$ i.e.,
$h = 18 \text{ cm}$ and $2\pi r = 30$ or $r =  rac{30}{2\pi} =  rac{30 	imes 7}{2 	imes 22} =  rac{105}{22}$.
Volume = $\pi r^2 h =  rac{22}{7} 	imes  rac{105}{22} 	imes  rac{105}{22} 	imes 18 \text{ cm}^3 =  rac{14175}{11} \text{ cm}^3$.
The cylinder formed by rolling the paper along its breadth has height $30 \text{ cm}$ and circumference of base $18 \text{ cm}$ i.e.,
$h = 30 \text{ cm}$ and $2\pi r = 18$ or $r =  rac{18}{2\pi} =  rac{18 	imes 7}{2 	imes 22} =  rac{63}{22}$.
Volume = $\pi r^2 h =  rac{22}{7} 	imes  rac{63}{22} 	imes  rac{63}{22} 	imes 30 \text{ cm}^3 =  rac{8505}{11} \text{ cm}^3$.
Required ratio = $ rac{14175}{11} :  rac{8505}{11} = 5 : 3$.

questions.question_text: Three rectangles $A_1$, $A_2$ and $A_3$ have the same area. Their lengths $a_1$, $a_2$ and $a_3$ respectively are such that $a_1 < a_2 < a_3$. Cylinders $C_1$, $C_2$ and $C_3$ are formed from $A_1$, $A_2$ and $A_3$ respectively by joining the parallel sides along the breadth. Then
questions.option_d: Each of $C_1$, $C_2$ and $C_3$ will enclose equal volume
questions.solution:
Let the breadths of the rectangles $A_1, A_2$ and $A_3$ be $b_1, b_2$ and $b_3$ respectively. Since the rectangles have the same area and $a_1 < a_2 < a_3$, we have: $b_1 > b_2 > b_3$.
When the rectangles are folded to form cylinders, then their lengths $a_1, a_2, a_3$ determine the radii of the cylinders while their breadths $b_1, b_2, b_3$ form their heights.
Volume of cylinder = $\pi r^2 h$.
Clearly, the rectangle $A_3$ with length $a_3$ shall have maximum value of $r^2$ and hence $C_3$ has maximum volume.

questions.question_text: The volume of a right circular cylinder whose curved surface area is 2640 cm$^2$ and circumference of its base is 66 cm, is
questions.solution:
$2\pi r = 66 \Rightarrow r =  rac{66 	imes 7}{2 	imes 22} = 21 \text{ cm}$.
$h =  rac{2640}{2\pi r} =  rac{2640}{66} = 40 \text{ cm}$.
Volume = $\frac{22}{7} 	imes 21 	imes 21 	imes 40 \text{ cm}^3 = 13860 \text{ cm}^3$.

questions.question_text: A well has to be dug out that is to be 22.5 m deep and of diameter 7m. Find the cost of plastering the inner curved surface at ₹ 3 per sq. meter.
questions.solution:
Curved surface area = $2\pi rh = 2 	imes  rac{22}{7} 	imes  rac{7}{2} 	imes 22.5 \text{ m}^2 = 495 \text{ m}^2$.
Cost of plastering = ₹ $(495 	imes 3) = ₹ 1485$.

questions.question_text: The radius and height of a cylinder are in the ratio $5 : 7$ and its volume is 4400 cm$^3$. Then its radius will be
questions.solution:
Let the radius and height of the cylinder be $5x$ and $7x \text{ cm}$ respectively.
Then, volume = $\pi r^2 h =  rac{22}{7} 	imes (5x)^2 	imes 7x = 550x^3 \text{ cm}^3$.
$550x^3 = 4400 \Rightarrow x^3 =  rac{4400}{550} = 8 \Rightarrow x = 2$.
Hence, radius = $(5 	imes 2) \text{ cm} = 10 \text{ cm}$.

questions.question_text: The height of a right circular cylinder is 14 cm and its curved surface is 704 sq. cm. Then its volume is:
questions.solution:
$\frac{2\pi rh}{h} =  rac{704}{14} \Rightarrow 2\pi r =  rac{704}{14} \Rightarrow r =  rac{704}{14} 	imes  rac{1}{2} 	imes  rac{7}{22} = 8 \text{ cm}$.
Volume = $\frac{22}{7} 	imes 8 	imes 8 	imes 14 \text{ cm}^3 = 2816 \text{ cm}^3$.

questions.question_text: A closed metallic cylindrical box is 1.25 m high and its base radius is 35 cm. If the sheet metal costs ₹ 80 per m$^2$, the cost of the material used in the box is
questions.solution:
Total surface area = $2\pi r(h+r) = 2 	imes  rac{22}{7} 	imes  rac{35}{100} 	imes (1.25 + 0.35) \text{ m}^2 = 2 	imes  rac{22}{7} 	imes  rac{35}{100} 	imes 1.6 \text{ m}^2 = 3.52 \text{ m}^2$.
Cost of the material = ₹ $(3.52 	imes 80) = ₹ 281.60$.

questions.question_text: The curved surface area of a right circular cylinder of base radius $r$ is obtained by multiplying its volume by
questions.solution: Curved surface area = $2\pi rh = \pi r^2 \cdot \frac{2}{r} = \text{Volume} \times \frac{2}{r}$.

questions.question_text: The ratio of total surface area to lateral surface area of a cylinder whose radius is 20 cm and height 60 cm, is
questions.solution: $\frac{\text{Total surface area}}{\text{Lateral surface area}} = \frac{2\pi r(h+r)}{2\pi rh} = \frac{h+r}{h} = \frac{80}{60} = \frac{4}{3}$.

questions.question_text: Two cans have the same height equal to 21 cm. One can is cylindrical, the diameter of whose base is 10 cm. The other can has square base of side 10 cm. What is the difference in their capacities?
questions.solution:
Difference in capacities = Volume of cuboidal can - Volume of cylindrical can
$= (10 	imes 10 	imes 21) - ( rac{22}{7} 	imes 5 	imes 5 	imes 21) \text{ cm}^3 = (2100 - 1650) \text{ cm}^3 = 450 \text{ cm}^3$.

questions.question_text: The diameter of the base of a cylindrical drum is 35 dm and the height is 24 dm. It is full of kerosene. How many tins each of size 25 cm $\times$ 22 cm $\times$ 35 cm can be filled with kerosene from the drum?
questions.solution: Number of tins = $\frac{\text{Volume of the drum}}{\text{Volume of each tin}} = \frac{\frac{22}{7} 	imes 35 	imes 35 	imes 24}{\frac{22}{7} 	imes  rac{25}{10} 	imes  rac{25}{10} 	imes  rac{35}{10}} = 1200$.

questions.question_text: The radius of the cylinder is half its height and area of the inner part is 616 sq. cms. Approximately how many litres of milk can it contain?
questions.solution:
It is given that $r = \frac{1}{2}h$ and $2\pi rh + \pi r^2 = 616 \text{ m}^2$.
$2\pi 	imes  rac{1}{2}h 	imes h + \pi 	imes  rac{1}{4}h^2 = 616 \Rightarrow \frac{5}{4}\pi h^2 = 616 \Rightarrow h^2 = 616 	imes  rac{4}{5} 	imes  rac{7}{22} = 78.4 \Rightarrow h = 28 \text{ m}$.
Volume = $\pi r^2 h = \frac{22}{7} 	imes 14 	imes 14 	imes 28 \text{ cm}^3 = 1.53 \text{ litres}$.

questions.question_text: The sum of the radius of the base and the height of a solid cylinder is 37 metres. If the total surface area of the cylinder be 1628 sq. metres, its volume is
questions.solution:
$(h+r)=37$ and $2	imes rac{22}{7}	imes r(h+r)=1628$. $\therefore 2	imes rac{22}{7}	imes r	imes37=1628$
or $r= rac{1628	imes7}{2	imes37	imes22}=7$.
So, $r=7$ m and $h=30$ m.
$\therefore$ Volume = $ rac{22}{7}	imes7	imes7	imes30$ m$^3$ = $4620$ m$^3$.

questions.question_text: The curved surface area of a cylindrical pillar is 264 m$^2$ and its volume is 924 m$^3$. Find the ratio of its diameter to its height.
questions.solution:
$\frac{\pi r^2h}{2\pi rh} = \frac{924}{264} \Rightarrow \frac{r}{2} = \frac{924}{264}=7$ m. And, $2\pi rh=264$
$\Rightarrow h = \left(264\times\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{7}{22}\times\frac{1}{7}\right)=6$ m.
$\therefore$ Required ratio = $\frac{2r}{h} = \frac{14}{6} = 7:3$.

questions.question_text: The height of a right circular cylinder is 6 m. If three times the sum of the areas of its two circular faces is twice the area of the curved surface, then the radius of its base is
questions.solution: $3\times2\pi r^2 = 2\times2\pi rh \Rightarrow 6r = 4h \Rightarrow r = \frac{4h}{6} = \left(\frac{2}{3}\times6\right)$ m = $4$ m.

questions.question_text: The height of a closed cylinder of given volume and the minimum surface area is
questions.solution:
$V = \pi r^2h$ and $S = 2\pi rh + 2\pi r^2 \Rightarrow S = 2\pi r(h+r)$,
where $h = \frac{V}{\pi r^2} \Rightarrow S = 2\pi r\left(\frac{V}{\pi r^2} + r\right) = \frac{2V}{r} + 2\pi r^2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{dS}{dr} = \frac{-2V}{r^2} + 4\pi r$ and $\frac{d^2S}{dr^2} = \left(\frac{4V}{r^3} + 4\pi\right) > 0$
$\therefore S$ is minimum when $\frac{dS}{dr} = 0 \Leftrightarrow \frac{2V}{r^2} = 4\pi r \Leftrightarrow V = 2\pi r^3 \Leftrightarrow \pi r^2h = 2\pi r^3 \Leftrightarrow h = 2r$.

questions.question_text: If the radius of the base of a right circular cylinder is halved, keeping the height same, what is the ratio of the volume of the reduced cylinder to that of the original one?
questions.solution:
Let original radius = $R$. Then, new radius = $\frac{R}{2}$.
Volume of reduced cylinder = $\frac{\pi\times(\frac{R}{2})^2\times h}{\pi R^2h} = \frac{1}{4}$.

questions.question_text: The radii of the bases of two cylinders are in the ratio $3 : 4$ and their heights are in the ratio $4 : 3$. The ratio of their volumes is
questions.solution:
Let their radii be $3x, 4x$ and heights be $4y, 3y$.
Ratio of their volumes = $\frac{\pi(3x)^2\times4y}{\pi(4x)^2\times3y} = \frac{36}{48} = 3:4$.

questions.question_text: If the height of a cylinder is increased by 15 percent and the radius of its base is decreased by 10 percent then by what percent will its curved surface area change?
questions.solution:
Let original height = $h$ and original radius = $r$.
New height = $115\%$ of $h = \frac{23}{20}h$.
New radius = $90\%$ of $r = \frac{9}{10}r$.
Original curved surface area = $2\pi rh$.
New curved surface area = $2\pi\times\frac{9}{10}r\times\frac{23}{20}h = \frac{207}{200}\times2\pi rh$.
Increase in curved surface area = $\left(\frac{207}{200} - 1\right)2\pi rh = \frac{7}{200}\times2\pi rh$.
$\therefore$ Increase% = $\left(\frac{7}{200}\times2\pi rh \times \frac{1}{2\pi rh}\times100\right)\% = 3.5\%$.

questions.question_text: If two cylinders of equal volumes have their heights in the ratio $2 : 3$, then the ratio of their radii is
questions.solution:
Let their heights be $2h$ and $3h$ and radii be $r$ and $R$ respectively. Then,
$\pi r^2(2h) = \pi R^2(3h) \Rightarrow \frac{r^2}{R^2} = \frac{3}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{r}{R} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}$ i.e. $\sqrt{3}:\sqrt{2}$.

questions.question_text: X and Y are two cylinders of the same height. The base of X has diameter that is half the diameter of the base of Y. If the height of X is doubled, the volume of X becomes
questions.option_a: equal to the volume of Y
questions.option_d: greater than the volume of Y
questions.solution:
Let the height of $X$ and $Y$ be $h$, and their radii be $r$ and $2r$ respectively. Then,
Volume of $X = \pi r^2h$ and Volume of $Y = \pi(2r)^2h = 4\pi r^2h$.
New height of $X = 2h$.
So, new volume of $X = \pi r^2(2h) = 2\pi r^2h = \frac{1}{2}(\text{Volume of } Y)$.

questions.question_text: The radius of a wire is decreased to one-third and its volume remains the same. The new length is how many times the original length?
questions.solution:
Let original radius = $r$ and original length = $h$.
New radius = $\frac{r}{3}$ and let new length = $H$. Then,
$\pi r^2h = \pi(\frac{r}{3})^2H$ or $H = 9h$.

questions.question_text: If the radius of a cylinder is decreased by 50% and the height is increased by 50% to form a new cylinder, the volume will be decreased by
questions.solution:
Let original radius = $r$ and original height = $h$. Original volume = $\pi r^2h$.
New radius = $50\%$ of $r = \frac{r}{2}$.
New height = $150\%$ of $h = \frac{3h}{2}$.
New volume = $\pi(\frac{r}{2})^2\times\frac{3h}{2} = \pi\times\frac{r^2}{4}\times\frac{3h}{2} = \frac{3}{8}\pi r^2h$.
Decrease in volume = $\pi r^2h - \frac{3}{8}\pi r^2h = \frac{5}{8}\pi r^2h$.
$\therefore$ Decrease% = $\left(\frac{5}{8}\pi r^2h \times \frac{1}{\pi r^2h}\times100\right)\% = 62.5\%$.

questions.question_text: Diameter of a jar cylindrical in shape is increased by 25%. By what percent must the height be decreased so that there is no change in its volume?
questions.solution:
Let original radius = $r$ and original height = $h$.
Original volume = $\pi r^2h$.
New radius = $125\%$ of $r = \frac{5r}{4}$. Let new height = $H$.
Then, $\pi r^2h = \pi(\frac{5r}{4})^2\times H \Rightarrow H = \frac{16}{25}h$.
Decrease in height = $h - \frac{16}{25}h = \frac{9}{25}h$.
$\therefore$ Decrease% = $\left(\frac{9}{25}h \times \frac{1}{h}\times100\right)\% = 36\%$.

questions.question_text: A cylindrical tank of diameter 35 cm is full of water. If 11 litres of water is drawn off, the water level in the tank will drop by
questions.solution:
Let the drop in the water level be $h$ cm. Then,
$\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{35}{2}\times\frac{35}{2}\times h = 11000 \Rightarrow h = \frac{11000\times7\times4}{22\times35\times35}$ cm
$= \frac{80}{7}$ cm = $11\frac{3}{7}$ cm.

questions.question_text: A well with inner diameter 8 m is dug 14 m deep. Earth taken out of its has been evenly spread all around it to a width of 3 m to form an embankment. The height of the embankment will be
questions.solution:
Volume of earth dug out = $\left(\frac{22}{7}\times4\times4\times14\right)$ m$^3$ = $704$ m$^3$.
Area of embankment = $\frac{22}{7}(7^2 - 4^2) = \frac{22}{7}\times11\times3$ m$^2$ = $\frac{726}{7}$ m$^2$.
Height of embankment = $\left(\frac{704}{726/7}\right)$ m = $\frac{704\times7}{726}$ m = $\frac{224}{33}$ m = $6\frac{26}{33}$ m.

questions.question_text: Water flows through a cylindrical pipe of internal diameter 7 cm at 2 m per second. If the pipe is always half full, then what is the volume of water (in litres) discharged in 10 minutes?
questions.solution:
Volume of water flown in 1 sec. = $\left(\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{7}{2}\times\frac{7}{2}\times200\right)$ cm$^3$ = $7700$ cm$^3$.
Volume of water flown in 10 min. = $(7700\times60\times10)$ cm$^3$
$= \frac{7700\times600}{1000}$ litres = $4620$ litres.

questions.question_text: The radius of a cylindrical cistern is 10 metres and its height is 15 metres. Initially the cistern is empty. We start filling the cistern with water through a pipe whose diameter is 50 cm. Water is coming out of the pipe with a velocity of 5 m/sec. How many minutes will it take in filling the cistern with water?
questions.solution:
Volume of cistern = $(\pi\times10^2\times15)$ m$^3$ = $1500\pi$ m$^3$.
Volume of water flowing through the pipe in 1 sec. = $(\pi\times0.25\times0.25\times5)$ m$^3$ = $0.3125\pi$ m$^3$.
$\therefore$ Time taken to fill the cistern = $\left(\frac{1500\pi}{0.3125\pi}\right)$ sec
$= 4800$ sec = $\frac{4800}{60}$ min = $80$ min.

questions.question_text: It is required to fix a pipe such that water flowing through it at a speed of 7 metres per minute fills a tank of capacity 440 cubic metres in 10 minutes. The inner radius of the pipe should be
questions.solution:
Let the inner radius of the pipe be $r$ metres. Then,
Volume of water flowing through the pipe in 10 min
$= \left(\frac{22}{7}\times r^2\times10\right)$ m$^3$ = $(220r^2)$ m$^3$.
$\therefore 220r^2 = 440 \Rightarrow r^2 = 2 \Rightarrow r = \sqrt{2}$ m.

questions.question_text: Water flows out through a circular pipe whose internal diameter is 2 cm, at the rate of 6 metres per second into a cylindrical tank, the radius of whose base is 60 cm. By how much will the level of water rise in 30 minutes?
questions.solution:
Volume of water flown through the pipe in 30 min
$= [(\pi\times0.01\times0.01\times6)\times30\times60]$ m$^3$ = $(1.08\pi)$ m$^3$.
Let the rise in level of water be $h$ metres.
Then, $\pi\times0.6\times0.6\times h = 1.08\pi \Rightarrow h = \frac{1.08}{0.6\times0.6}$ m = $3$ m.

questions.question_text: Water is poured into an empty cylindrical tank at a constant rate for 5 minutes. After the water has been poured into the tank, the depth of the water is 7 feet. The radius of the tank is 100 feet. Which of the following is the best approximation for the rate at which the water was poured into the tank?
questions.solution:
Volume of water flown into the tank in 5 min.
$= \left(\frac{22}{7}\times100\times100\times7\right)$ cu. feet = $2220000$ cu. feet.
$\therefore$ Rate of flow of water = $\frac{2200000}{5\times60}$ cu. ft/sec
$= 733.3$ cu. ft/sec $\approx 700$ cu. ft/sec.

questions.question_text: The number of circular pipes with an inside diameter of 1 inch which will carry the same amount of water as a pipe with an inside diameter of 6 inches is
questions.solution:
Let the length of each pipe be $l$ inches.
Then, volume of water in thinner pipe
$= \left[\pi\times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2\times l\right]$ cu. inch = $\frac{\pi l}{4}$ cu. inch.
Volume of water in thicker pipe = $(\pi\times3^2\times l)$ cu.inch = $(9\pi l)$ cu.inch.
$\therefore$ Required number of pipes = $\frac{9\pi l}{\pi l/4} = 36$.

questions.question_text: Find the number of coins 1.5 cm in diameter and 0.2 cm thick, to be melted to form a right circular cylinder of height 10 cm and diameter 4.5 cm.
questions.solution:
Volume of one coin = $\left(\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{75}{100}\times\frac{75}{100}\times\frac{2}{10}\right) \text{cm}^3 = \frac{99}{280} \text{cm}^3$.
Volume of larger cylinder = $\left(\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{9}{4}\times\frac{9}{4}\times\frac{10}{1}\right) \text{cm}^3$.
Number of coins = $\left(\frac{22}{7}\times\frac{9}{4}\times\frac{9}{4}\times\frac{10}{1}\times\frac{280}{99}\right) = 450$.

questions.question_text: Two cylindrical vessels with radii 15 cm and 10 cm and heights 35 cm and 15 cm respectively are filled with water. If this water is poured into a cylindrical vessel 15 cm in height, then the radius of the vessel is
questions.solution:
Let the radius of the vessel be $R$. Then,
$\pi R^2 \times 15 = \pi \times (15)^2 \times 35 + \pi \times (10)^2 \times 15$
$\Leftrightarrow \pi R^2 \times 15 = 9375\pi$
